questions.explanation: Women of Saudi Arabia used their right to vote first time.

questions.question: When was the Legal Framework Order passed by Pervez Musharraf in Pakistan?
questions.explanation: Legal Framework Order was passed by Pervez Musharraf in August 2002. According to this order, the President can dismiss the national or provincial assemblies.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not the argument against democracy?

options.html: Rule of law and respect for rights
Democracy is all about political competition and power play.

questions.explanation: This one is not argument against democracy. It is the feature of democracy.

questions.question: Who declared himself the Chief Executive of the country in Pakistan in 1999?
questions.explanation: In Pakistan, General Pervez Musharraf led the military coup in October 1999. He overthrew a democratically elected Government and declared himself the 'Chief Executive' of the country.

questions.question: What is the name of Chinese parliament?
questions.explanation: Chinese parliament is called Quanguo Renmin Daibiao Dahui(National People's Congress)

questions.question: What are the features of democracy?

options.html: One person, one vote, one value

questions.explanation: These all are the features of a good democracy. Without these features one country cannot be a good democratic country.

questions.question: During which period did China record its worst famines in the world's history?
questions.explanation: China’s famine of 1958-1961 was the worst recorded famine in world history. Nearly three crore people died in this famine.

questions.question: Which party of Zimbabwe helped its country to gain independence?
questions.explanation: Zimbabwe attained independence from White minority rule in 1980. Since then the country has been ruled by ZANU-PF, the party that led the freedom struggle.

questions.question: Who did not have the right to vote in Saudi Arabia?
questions.explanation: There are many instances of denial of equal right to vote. For example: In Saudi Arabia, women did not have the right to vote. Women were previously forbidden from voting in all elections or being elected to any political office, but in 2011 King Abdullah allowed women to vote in the 2015 local municipal elections and be appointed to the Consultative Assembly.

questions.question: The famous political party of Mexico was:
questions.explanation: In Mexico, until 2000 every election was won by a party called PRI (Institutional Revolutionary Party)

questions.question: Which Pakistan General led a military coup in October 1999?
questions.explanation: In Pakistan, General Pervez Musharraf led a military coup in October 1999.

questions.question: Who was the Prime Minister of Zimbabwe?
questions.explanation: Robert mugabe was the president of Zimbabwe since 1980 to 2017. He was the leader of ZANU-PF party.

questions.question: The word Democracy comes from the Greek word__________
questions.explanation: The word 'democracy' has its origins in the Greek language. It combines two shorter words: 'demos' meaning people and ‘kratia’ meaning power or rule. . So democracy is a rule by the people. This is the correct meaning.

tests.instructionHtml:
Students are instructed to read all the points carefully before clicking the START TEST button.

General Instructions:

  • Students must not use calculators and any other unfair means while taking the test.
  • The duration of the test is 30 minutes. You will not be able to submit the test after the time is over.
  • There will be total 15 MCQ in this test.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: What is the date of adoption of the Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly that wrote the Indian constitution had 299 members. The Assembly adopted the Constitution on 26th November 1949 but it came into effect on 26th January 1950. To mark this day we celebrate January 26 as Republic Day every year.

questions.question: Which country has the lengthiest Constitution in the world?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India is the longest written constitution of any country in the world, containing 444 articles in 22 parts, 12 schedules and 124 amendments. It covers all the details and descriptions of all the powers which are enjoyed by the executive, legislature and judiciary.

questions.question: Among the following who was the chairman of Constitutional Drafting Committee?
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly which was constituted in November 1946 comprised of several Committees. The Drafting Committee headed by Dr B.R. Ambedkar was entrusted with the drafting of the Indian Constitution. It had 6 other members.

questions.question: When Motilal Nehru and eight other Congress leaders drafted a constitution for India?
questions.explanation: The Constitution drafted in 1928 was known as the Nehru Report. It was prepared by a committee of All Parties Conference chaired by Motilal Nehru with Jawaharlal Nehru as its Secretary. There were a total of 9 members in the Committee.

questions.question: Mention the main leader of South Africa who opposed Apartheid.
questions.explanation:
• Nelson Mandela and seven other leaders were sentenced to life imprisonment in 1964, for daring to oppose the apartheid regime in his country.
  • He spent the next 28 years in Robben Island, South Africa’s most dreaded prison.
  • He was released after 28 years from jail, and at midnight of the 26th of April 1994, South Africa got independence. He became the first President of independent South Africa.

questions.question: Which party fought the liberation movement in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Since the end of apartheid in 1994, the African National Congress (ANC) has dominated South Africa's politics. The ANC is the ruling party in the national legislature, as well as in eight of the nine provinces (Western Cape is governed by the Democratic Alliance).

questions.question: Which country became democratic on 26 April 1994?
questions.explanation: South Africa become a democratic country on 26 April 1994.

questions.question: Which of these did not form a part of the changed attitude of South African government?

options.html: Nelson Mandela was freed from imprisonment
Ban on political parties and media was lifted

questions.explanation: More discriminatory laws were passed.

questions.question: Which of these positions is correct in relation to the Sovereign status of India?

options.html: Pakistan can control India’s Armed Forces
USA can decide India’s foreign policy
The Indian government only can decide its internal and external policies
USSR can support the CPI (M) in setting up its government here

questions.explanation: The Indian government only can decide its internal and external policies.

questions.question:
The Indian constitution has borrowed from:

  1. Ideas from the French Revolution.
  2. The British Constitution.
  3. The Bill of Rights of the US.
  4. Israeli Constitution.
questions.explanation: i, ii, iii

questions.question: What did the black population want in the new Constitution?

options.html: Whites should be turned out of the country

questions.explanation: Substantial social and economic rights.

questions.question: Where was the 1931 session of the Indian National Congress held?
questions.explanation: Karachi

questions.question: Name the organization that led the struggle against the policies of segregation.
questions.explanation: African National Congress

questions.question: Name the autobiography of Nelson Mandela.
questions.explanation: The Long Walk to Freedom

questions.question: On what charges was Nelson Mandela sentenced to life imprisonment?
questions.explanation: For treason

questions.question: Each citizen should respect the spirit of brotherhood and no one should treat a fellow citizen as inferior. Choose one word for this statement?
questions.explanation: Fraternity

questions.question: The land of the law would not discriminate between citizens on the basis of caste, religion, and gender. Choose one word for this statement?
questions.explanation: Equality

questions.question: This term means that the head of the state, i.e. the President of India is an elected person and it is not a hereditary position. Choose the term from options.
questions.explanation: Republic

questions.question: The ________ means that India is a free country and no external power can dictate the government of India.
questions.explanation: Sovereignty

questions.question: India would adopt a form of government in which people would elect their rulers and hold them accountable. Choose one word for this?
questions.explanation: Democratic

questions.question: All citizens are equal before the law; irrespective of differences in socio-economic conditions. Choose one word for this statement?
questions.explanation: All citizens are equal before the law; irrespective of differences in socio-economic conditions.

questions.question: There is no official religion in India and no religion gets special status from the government of India. All religions are treated with equal respect by the government. Choose one word for this statement?
questions.explanation: Secular

questions.question: Elections to the Constituent Assembly were held in ________.
questions.explanation: Elections to the Constituent Assembly were held in July 1946.

questions.question: On which day, democracy as a new form of Government was adopted in South Africa?
The leaders who participated in the making of our Indian Constitution are inspired by which Revolution?
questions.explanation:
From French Revolution 1789-1799:

  1. The ideal of Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity.
  2. The ideal of Republic.

questions.question: Which of the following values, not mentioned in preamble?
questions.explanation:
The following are the values mentioned in the preamble:
Sovereignty, Socialism, Secularism, Democratic and republic nature of India, Justice, Liberty, Equality and Fraternity.

questions.question: Which country has the longest written constitution?
questions.explanation: India

questions.question: The Constitution begins with a short statement of its basic values. What is it called?
questions.explanation: Preamble

questions.question: According to which Act were the elections held to provincial legislatures in India in 1937?
questions.explanation: Government of India Act, 1935

questions.question: How many days the Constituent Assembly spent on the consideration to draft the constitution?

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: When was the Constitution of India adopted?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted on 26 Nov. 1949 and came to force on 26 Jan. 1950.

questions.question: Who was the chairman of the drafting committee of Indian constitution
questions.explanation: On 29 August, 1947, the Constituent Assembly set up a Drafting Committee,under the Chairmanship of Dr. B.R. Ambedkar to prepare a Draft Constitution for India.

questions.question: The Constitution of India is
questions.explanation: For the purpose of amendment, our constitution has been divided into three parts, certain provisions of the constitution can be amended by a simple majority in Parliament, others by a 2/3 majority and yet others by 2/3 majority in Parliament plus its ratification by at least fifty percent states.

questions.question: Which among the following is not a feature of the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India provides for single citizenship-citizenship of India. There is no separate state citizenship.

questions.question: Mahatma Gandhi’s vision about the Indian Constitution was published in which magazine?
questions.explanation: Young India was a weekly paper or journal in English published by Mahatma Gandhi from 1919 to 1931.

questions.question: What is The Long Walk of Freedom?

options.html: A magazine sponsored by Nelson Mandela .

questions.explanation: It is the autobiography of Nelson Mandela.

questions.question: Which one of the following guiding values of the Constitution of India means, that the head of the state is an elected person and not a hereditary position?
questions.explanation: Republic means the head of the state is an elected person and not a hereditary position.

questions.question: Which one of the following guiding values of the Constitution of India means A form of a Government where people enjoy equal political rights, elect their rulers and hold them accountable?
questions.explanation: Democracy is a form of a Government where people enjoy equal political rights, elect their rulers and hold them accountable.

questions.question: Which one of the following guiding values of the Constitution of India means All of us should behave like we are a member of the same family?
questions.explanation: It means all of us should behave like we are the member of the same family.

questions.question: Which one of the following guiding values of the Constitution of India means all are equal below the law?
questions.explanation: It means means all all equal below the law.

questions.question: Which one of the following guiding values of the Constitution of India means people have complete freedom to follow any religion?
questions.explanation: It mean freedom to follow any religion

questions.question: Which one of the following guiding values of Constitution of India means people have supreme right to make decisions?
questions.explanation: Sovereign means people have the supreme right to take decisions.

questions.question: Who was the first Education Minister of free India?
questions.explanation: Abul Kalam Azad was the first Education minister of free India.

questions.question: Who among the following was not a member of the constituent assembly?
questions.explanation: Mahatma Gandhi was not a member of the constituent assembly.

questions.question: What is meant by the term Socialist?

options.html: Wealth is generated socially and should be distributed equally in the society.
Wealth should be distributed to the rich section of the society.
Wealth should be distributed to the poor only.
No one will get the benefit of the society.

questions.explanation: Socialist means where Wealth is created and should be distributed equally in the society.

questions.question: What is the color of the native people of South Africa?
questions.explanation: The color of the native people of South Africa is Black.

questions.question: In which year Nelson Mandela was sentenced to Life Imprisonment?
questions.explanation: In the year of 1964 Nelson Mandela was sentenced to Life Imprisonment.

questions.question: In which year South Africa became independent?
questions.explanation: 26th April 1994 South Africa became independent

questions.question: What is Apartheid?
questions.explanation: Apartheid means Racial Discrimintaion

questions.question: What is the full form of EPIC?
questions.explanation: Full form of EPIC is Election Photo Identity Card

questions.question: Who was commonly known as the Iron Man of India?
questions.explanation: Sardar Vallabbhai Patel was commonly known as the Iron Man of India.

questions.question: Who was the president of the constituent assembly?
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India.

questions.question: Which of these is a provision that a democratic constitution does not have?

options.html: Name of the head of the state
Powers of the head of the state

questions.explanation: Name of the head of the state is not included in the constitution which is democratic

questions.question: Which of these was the most salient underlying conflict in the making of a democratic constitution in South Africa?

options.html: Between the white majority and the black minority
Between the colored minority and the black majority
Between South Africa and its neighbors.

questions.explanation: The coloured people in South Africa are a mixed race of people usually half Khoisan and half European. These people were minority and Blacks in South Africa were majority. There was a conflict between these two groups and this led to the establishment of a democratic government in South Africa.

questions.question: A form of government where people enjoy equal political rights is:
questions.explanation: Democratic

questions.question: The Chairman of the drafting committee was:
questions.explanation: A Drafting Committee chaired by Dr. B.R. Ambedkar prepared a draft constitution for discussion.

questions.question: When was the Constitution of India completed?
questions.explanation: The Assembly adopted the Constitution on 26 November 1949 but it came into effect on 26 January 1950.

questions.question: How many members were there in the Constituent Assembly that wrote the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: The Assembly adopted the Constitution on 26 November 1949 but it came into effect on 26 January 1950. The Constituent Assembly that wrote the Indian constitution had 299 members.

questions.question: Nelson Mandela remained in prison for treason for about:
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela spent the next 27 years in South Africa’s most dreaded prison, Robben Island.

questions.question: Among the following who was the chairman of Constitutional Drafting Committee?
questions.explanation: The Constituent Assembly which was constituted in November 1946 comprised of several Committees. The Drafting Committee headed by Dr B.R. Ambedkar was entrusted with the drafting of the Indian Constitution. It had 6 other members.

questions.question: Which political party had given the slogan "Save Democracy"?
questions.explanation: Save democracy was the slogan given by Janata Party in the Lok Sabha election held in 1977.

questions.question: Who had given the slogan Garibi Hatao?
questions.explanation: Garibi Hatao ("Remove poverty") was the theme and slogan of Indira Gandhi's 1971 election campaign.

questions.question: Who had formed the Lok Dal Party in Haryana?
questions.explanation: Formation of new Party Lok Dal Haryana has been ruled by Congress party since 1982 at that time Chaudhary Devi Lal was an opposition leader called Narayana yodh and formed a new party, Lok Dal.

questions.question: Fraud and malpractices indulged by a party or candidate to increase its votes are called ________.
questions.explanation: Rigging: Fraud and malpractices indulged by a party or candidate to increase its votes. It includes stuffing ballot boxes by a few persons using the votes of others, recording multiple votes by the same person and bribing or coercing polling officers to favour a candidate.

questions.question: What is the minimum age for being a member of the parliament?
questions.explanation: The minimum age is 25 years in order to be a candidate.

questions.question: ________ is a sheet of paper on which the names of the contesting candidates along with party name and symbols are listed.
questions.explanation: A ballot paper is a sheet of paper on which the names of the contesting candidates along with party name and symbols are listed.

questions.question: EPIC stands for?
questions.explanation: Election Photo Identity Card [EPIC]

questions.question: Electoral Roll is commonly known as the ________.
questions.explanation: Electoral Roll is commonly known as the Voters’ List.

questions.question: ________ entitles every citizen to elect her/his representative and to be elected as a representative.
questions.explanation: India's Constitution entitles every citizen to elect her/his representative and to be elected as a representative.

questions.question: Elections are held in all constituencies at the same time, either on the same day or within a few days. This is called a ________.
questions.explanation: Elections are held in all constituencies at the same time, either on the same day or within a few days. This is called a general election.

questions.question: The election that is held only for one constituency to fill the vacancy caused by death or resignation of a member is called ________.
questions.explanation: An Election that is held only for one constituency to fill the vacancy caused by death or resignation of a member is called as a by-election.

questions.question: The Lok Sabha or Vidhan Sabha stands dissolved after how many years?
questions.explanation: After five years the term of all the elected representatives comes to an end. The Lok Sabha or Vidhan Sabha stands ‘dissolved’.

questions.question: EVM stands for?
questions.explanation: Electronic voting machines

questions.question: Who implements the Code of Conduct and punishes any candidate or party that violates it?
questions.explanation: EC implements the Code of Conduct and punishes any candidate or party that violates it.

questions.question: Which of the following is incorrect with respect to Election Commission?

options.html: EC takes decisions on every aspect of conduct and control of elections.
EC implements the Code of Conduct and punishes any candidate or party that violates it.
Elections are conducted by an independent and very powerful EC.
During the election period, the government can order the EC to follow some guidelines.

questions.explanation: During the election period, the EC can order the government to follow some guidelines.

questions.question: Which of the following is incorrect with respect to Election Commission?

options.html: EC implements the Code of Conduct and punishes any candidate or party that violates it.
During the election period, the government can order the EC to follow some guidelines.
Elections are conducted by an independent and very powerful EC.
EC takes decisions on every aspect of conduct and control of elections.

questions.explanation: During the election period, the EC can order the government to follow some guidelines.

questions.question: Who votes much less than the rich and the white people in USA?
questions.explanation: In the United States of America, poor people, African Americans and Hispanics vote much less than the rich and the white people.

questions.question: In which country underprivileged people vote in larger proportion as compared to the rich and privileged sections?
questions.explanation: In India, the poor, illiterate and underprivileged people vote in larger proportion as compared to the rich and privileged sections.

questions.question: In which country an incumbent or sitting elected representative rarely loses an election?
questions.explanation: In the USA, an incumbent or ‘sitting’ elected representative rarely loses an election.

questions.question: What is the current holder of a political office is called?
questions.explanation: Incumbent: The current holder of a political office.

questions.question: Name the party formed by Chaudhary Devi Lal?
questions.explanation: Chaudhary Devi Lal, an opposition leader, led a movement called ‘Nyaya Yudh’ (Struggle for Justice) and formed a new party, Lok Dal.

questions.question: Who led a movement called Nyaya Yudh?
questions.explanation: Chaudhary Devi Lal, an opposition leader, led a movement called ‘Nyaya Yudh’ (Struggle for Justice) and formed a new party, Lok Dal.

questions.question: Who said that if his party won the elections, his government would waive the loans of farmers and small businessmen?
questions.explanation: In the election campaign, Devi Lal said that if his party won the elections, his government would waive the loans of farmers and small businessmen.

questions.question: Choose the appropriate word - Supporters or hired musclemen of party or a candidate gain physical control of a polling booth and cast false votes by threatening everyone or by preventing genuine voters form reaching the polling booth.
questions.explanation: Booth capturing: Supporters or hired musclemen of party or a candidate gain physical control of a polling booth and cast false votes by threatening everyone or by preventing genuine voters from reaching the polling booth.

questions.question: Which of these is not a good reason to say that Indian elections are democratic?

options.html: In India, everyone above the age of 18 has a right to vote.
India’s Election Commission is very powerful.
India has the largest number of voters in the world.
In India, the losing parties accept the electoral verdict.

questions.explanation: India has the largest number of voters in the world. Many countries have a large number of voters and hence this can't be an acceptable reason to say that Indian elections are democratic.

questions.question: Here are some tatements about the reasons for conducting elections. Which of them are false?

options.html: Elections enable people to evaluate the performance of judiciary.
People can indicate which policies they prefer.
People select the representative of their choice in an election.
Elections enable people to judge the performance of the government.

questions.explanation: Elections enable people to evaluate the performance of the government.

questions.question: ________ indicates the percent of eligible voters who actually cast their vote.
questions.explanation: People’s participation in elections is usually measured by voter turnout figures. Turnout indicates the percent of eligible voters who actually cast their vote.

questions.question: Under the control of which of the following body the government officers work during election duty?
questions.explanation: When on election duty, government officers work under the control of the EC and not the government.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are not function of the Election Commissioner of India ?
A. EC takes decision on every aspect of conduct and control of elections.
B. It implements the code of conduct.
C. It issues election Manifesto.
questions.explanation:
EC does not issue Election Manifesto.

Hence Only A and B are true.

questions.question:
Which of the following is/are true with refernce to Chief Election Commissioner ?
A. He/She is appointed by the President.
B. Once appointed, he/she is not answerable to the President or the government.

options.html: A is true but B is false
Both A and B are false
Both A and B are true
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation:
The Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) is
appointed by the President of India. But once appointed, the Chief Election Commissioner is not answerable to the President or the government.

  • Questions can be answered in any order in given time.
  • For moving to previous question click on "PREVIOUS" at the bottom of the screen.
  • Students can mark the question for review too.
  • Students can make changes in their choice of options before clicking the "SUBMIT" button.
  • After completing all the questions, click "SUBMIT" button. A message will be displayed on the screen "Do you really want to submit ?". If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Who appoints the Chief Election Commissioner of India?
questions.explanation: The Chief Election Commissioner (CEC) is appointed by the President of India. But once appointed, the Chief Election Commissioner is not answerable to the President or the government.

questions.question: Who is responsible for free and fair elections in India?
questions.explanation: In our country elections are conducted by an independent and very powerful Election Commission (EC). EC is responsible for free and fair elections in India.

questions.question:
Arrange the following in the correct sequence.

  1. Counting of votes
  2. Election campaign
  3. Nomination of candidates
  4. Voters list
questions.explanation:
4, 3, 2, 1

  1. Once the constituencies are decided, the next step is to decide who can and who cannot vote. This is done with the making of Voter's List.
  2. Political parties nominate their candidates who get the party symbol and support. 
  3. Then the Election campaign is done. In our country, such campaigns take place for a two-week period between the announcement of the final list of candidates and the date of polling. 
  4. The final stage of an election is the day when the voters cast or ‘poll’ their vote and the counting is done.

questions.question: What is an EVM?
questions.explanation: Nowadays electronic voting machines (EVM) are used to record votes. The machine shows the names of the candidates and the party symbols. Independent candidates too have their own symbols, allotted by the election commission. All that the voter has to do is to press the button against the name of the candidate she wants to give her vote.

questions.question: Choose the appropriate word - It is a sheet of paper on which the names of the contesting candidates along with party name and symbol are listed.
questions.explanation: Earlier the voters used to indicate who they wanted to vote for by putting a stamp on the ballot paper. A ballot paper is a sheet of paper on which the names of the contesting candidates along with party name and symbols are listed.

questions.question: What is the code of conduct?

options.html: Conditions in which all parties contest elections.
A set of norms and guidelines to be followed by political parties.
Instructions issued by the Prime Minister.

questions.explanation: Code of Conduct: A set of norms and guidelines to be followed by political parties and contesting candidates during election time. All the political parties in our country have agreed to a Model Code of Conduct for election campaigns.

questions.question: Which party gave a slogan Garibi Hatao in 1971 elections?
questions.explanation: The Congress party led by Indira Gandhi gave the slogan of Garibi Hatao (Remove poverty) in the Lok Sabha elections of 1971.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to voter and the candidate ?
A. Anyone who can be a voter can also become a candidate.
B. Anyone who can be a voter but has attained the age fo 25 years can become a candidate.

options.html: Both A and B are false
A is true but B is false
Both A and B are true
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation: Anyone who can be a voter can also become a candidate in elections. The only difference is that in order to be a candidate the minimum age is 25 years, while it is only 18 years for being a voter.

questions.question: What is EPIC?
questions.explanation: In the last few years, a new system of Election Photo Identity Card [EPIC] has been introduced. The government has tried to give this card to every person on the voters' list. The voters are required to carry this card when they go out to vote so that no one can vote for someone else.

questions.question: Which of the following state has the highest number of constituencies in Lok sabha?
questions.explanation: Uttar Pradesh has the highest number of constituencies i.e 80

questions.question: Who is eligible to vote in India ? A. All the citizens aged 18 years of age B. All the citizens aged 18 years and above C. All the Indian citizens
questions.explanation: In our country, all the citizens aged 18 years and above can vote in an election. Every citizen has the right to vote, regardless of his or her caste, religion or gender.

questions.question:
Read the statement and choose the appropriate word -
It is the list of those who are eligible to vote.
questions.explanation: In a democratic election, the list of those who are eligible to vote is prepared much before the election and given to everyone. This list is officially called the Electoral Roll and is commonly known as the Voters’ List.

questions.question: How many seats are reserved for the ST in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: The 1st schedule to R. P. Act, 1950 as amended vide Representation of People. For Scheduled Tribes, 47 seats are reserved in Lok Sabha.

questions.question: How many seats are reserved for the SC in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanation: Currently, in the Lok Sabha, 84 seats are reserved for the Scheduled Castes.

questions.question: How many Lok Sabha Constituencies are there in India?
questions.explanation: The Lok Sabha, the lower house of the Parliament of India, is made up of Members of Parliament (MPs). Each represents a single geographic constituency. There are currently 543 constituencies.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question:
‘Constituency ‘A’ was declared as reserved constituency. What does this stands for ?
A. Only those who belong to SC and ST can caste their votes.
B. Only someone who belongs to the Scheduled Cast/Scheduled Tribe can contest the elections.

options.html: Both A and B are true
A is true but B is false
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation: The makers of our Constitution thought of a special system of reserved constituencies for the weaker sections. Some constituencies are reserved for people who belong to the Scheduled Castes [SC] and Scheduled Tribes [ST]. In a SC reserved constituency only someone who belongs to the Scheduled Castes can stand for election. Similarly only those belonging to the Scheduled Tribes can contest an election from a constituency reserved for ST.

questions.question:
‘Our Constitution makers thought of a special system of reserved constituencies. Which of the following is/are true with reference to this ?
A. They were worried that in an open electoral system of reserved constituencies certain weaker sections may not stand a good chance to get elected to the Lok Sabha and the state Legislative Assemblies.
B. The reservation was based on the economic development of the constituency.

options.html: Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true
A is true but B is false

questions.explanation: In our country we follow an area based system of representation. The country is divided into different areas for purposes of elections. These areas are called electoral constituencies. Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question:
Read the statement and choose the appropriate word -
Election is held only for one Constituency to fill the vacancy caused by death or resignation by a member.
questions.explanation: Sometimes an election is held only for one constituency to fill the vacancy caused by death or resignation of a member. This is called a by-election.

questions.question:
Read the statement and choose the appropriate word -
Elections are held in all Constituencies at the same time, either on the same day or within a few days.
questions.explanation: Elections are held in all constituencies at the same time, either on the same day or within a few days. This is called a general election.

questions.question:
Read the statement and choose the right word –
Voters in a geographical area who elect a representative to the legislative body.
questions.explanation: Elections are held in all constituencies at the same time,either on the same day or within a few days. Constituency means Voters in a geographical area who elect a representative to the legislative body.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to minimum condition of a democratic election ?
A. Everyone should be able to choose.
B. The candidate preferred by people should not get elected.

options.html: Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is true but B is false
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation:
A.There should be something to choose from. Parties and candidates should be free to contest elections and should offer some real choice to the voters.

B.The candidate preferred by the people should get elected.

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question:
Read the statement and choose the right word –
It is a mechanism by which people can choose their representative at regular intervals and change them if they wish to do so.
questions.explanation: A proper mechanism is required by which people can choose their representatives at regular intervals and change them if they wish to do so. This mechanism is called an election. Therefore, elections are considered essential in our times for any representative democracy.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important feature of democracy?
questions.explanation: Elections take place regularly in any democracy in a free and fair manner. Elections should be conducted in a free and fair manner where people can choose as they really wish.

questions.question: Who invites the leader of the majority party to form the state government after the State Assembly elections?
questions.explanation: After the State Assembly elections Governor invites the leader of the majority party to form the state government.

questions.question: Which of the following is right expansion of the word MLA ?
questions.explanation: MLA stands for Member of Legislative Assembly

questions.question: Which of the following party gave a slogan Nyaya Yudh in 1987?
questions.explanation: The State had been ruled by a Congress party-led government since 1982. Chaudhary Devi Lal, the then opposition leader, led a movement called ‘Nyaya Yudh’ (Struggle for Justice) and formed a new party, Lok Dal.

questions.question: One-third of the seats are reserved in rural and urban local bodies for ________ candidates.
questions.explanation: The proportion of seats reserved varies from state to state. Similarly,one-third of the seats are reserved in rural and urban local bodies for women candidates.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the reasons for conducting elections are false?

options.html: People select the representative of their choice in an election.
People can indicate which policies they prefer.
Elections enable people to judge the performance of the government.
Elections enable people to evaluate the performance of the judiciary.

questions.explanation: Elections enable people to evaluate the performance of the government.

questions.question: Who implements the Code of Conduct and punishes any candidate or party that violates it?
questions.explanation: EC implements the Code of Conduct and punishes any candidate or party that violates it.

questions.question: Who had formed the Lok Dal Party in Haryana?
questions.explanation: Formation of new Party Lok Dal Haryana has been ruled by Congress party since 1982 at that time Chaudhary Devi Lal was an opposition leader called Narayana yodh and formed a new party, Lok Dal.

If student click on "NO", the will be redirected to the test page again and if they press "YES", the result will be displayed on the screen with details of questions attempted.

questions.question: Which of the houses of Parliament is more powerful than others?
questions.explanation:
Lok Sabha may be considered to be the more powerful House of Parliament due to the following reasons:

  1. Money Bill can be introduced in the Lok Sabha only and not in Rajya Sabha and Rajya Sabha cannot do much to stop its passage. It can only delay it by 14 days.
  2. Any Ordinary law needs to be passed by both the houses, but if there is any difference between the two houses, the final decision is taken in a joint session of both the houses. Because of the large number of members, the view of the Lok Sabha is preferred.

questions.question: Which organ of the government makes laws in any country?
questions.explanation: The legislative branch makes the laws. It is is also known as the law-making body.

questions.question: Which government implemented the report of the Mandal Commission Report?
questions.explanation: In the Lok Sabha election of 1989, the Janata Dal promised that if voted to power, it would implement the Mandal Commission report. The Janata Dal did form the government after this election. Its leader V. P. Singh became the Prime Minister.

questions.question: Who headed the Second Backward Classes Commission in 1979?
questions.explanation: The Government of India had appointed the Second Backward Classes Commission in 1979. It was headed by B.P. Mandal. Hence it was popularly called the Mandal Commission.

questions.question: A procedure through which the High Court or the Supreme Court determine the Constitutional validity of any legislation -
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court and the High Courts have the power to interpret the Constitution of the country. They can declare invalid any law of the legislature or the actions of the executive, whether at the Union level or at the state level if they find such law or action is against the constitution. Thus they can determine the Constitutional validity of any legislation or action of the executive in the country when it is challenged before them. This is known as judicial review.

questions.question: How can a judge of the Supreme Court be removed?
questions.explanation: A judge can be removed only by an impeachment motion passed separately by two-thirds members of the two Houses of the Parliament.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to the appointment of the judges of the Supreme Court ?
A. The judges are appointed by the President.
B. The judges are appointed on the advice of the Prime Minister and in consultation with the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.

options.html: Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true
A is true but B is false

questions.explanation: The judges of the Supreme Court and the High Courts are appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister and in consultation with the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Which is the highest court of appeal?
questions.explanation: Supreme Court is the highest court of appeal in civil and criminal cases. It can hear appeals against the decisions of the High Courts.

questions.question: Choose the appropriate term: All the courts at different levels in a country put together in a country.
questions.explanation: An institution empowered to administer justice and provide a mechanism for the resolution of legal disputes. All the courts in the country are collectively referred to as judiciary.

questions.question:
What are the options with the President if a bill is sent to him/her for assent?
A. He/she has to sign it.
B. He/She can sent it back to the House for reconsideration.
C. If bill is sent to the President second time he/she has to sign it.

options.html: Only A and B are true.

questions.explanation:
A bill passed by the Parliament becomes a law only after the President gives assent to it. If the President wants,she can delay this for some time and send the bill back to the Parliament for reconsideration. But if the Parliament passes the bill again, she has to sign it.

Hence only B and C are true.

questions.question: Who is the Supreme Commander of the defence forces of India?
questions.explanation: All international treaties and agreements are made in the name of the President. The President is the supreme commander of the defence forces of India.

questions.question: Who appoints Chief Justice of India?
questions.explanation: All major appointments are made in the name of the President. These include the appointment of the Chief Justice of India, the Judges of the Supreme Court and the High Courts of the states, the Governors of the states, the Election Commissioners, ambassadors to other countries, etc. Hence, the Chief Justice of India is appointed by President.

questions.question:
Which of the following exercises only nominal powers ?
A. He is directly elected by the people.
B. He/She is nominal executive.

options.html: Both A and B are false
Both A and B are true
A is true but B is false
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation:
The President is the head of the State. In our political system the head of the State exercises only nominal powers. 

A.The President is not elected directly by the people. The elected Members of Parliament (MPs) and the elected Members of the Legislative Assemblies (MLAs) elect  president.
B.The President can never claim the kind of direct popular mandate that the Prime Minister can. This ensures that President remains only a nominal executive.

Hence A is False and B is True

questions.question: Which of the following exercises only nominal powers?
questions.explanation: The President is the head of the State. In our political system, the head of the State exercises only nominal powers.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to the resignation of Prime Minister ?
A. If he/she resigns the entire ministry quits.
B. The Prime Minister gives his resignation to the Speaker of the Lok Sabha.

options.html: A is true but B is false
Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation:
1.When the Prime Minister quits, the entire ministry quits.

2.Prime Minister holds his office until he enjoys the majority support in the Loksabha. When he loses the confidence of the Loksabha, he must resign by writing to the president.

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question: Who chairs Cabinet meetings?
questions.explanation: As head of the government, the Prime Minister has wide-ranging powers. He chairs Cabinet meetings. He coordinates the work of different Departments, etc.

questions.question: They are usually attached to and required to assist Cabinet Ministers -
questions.explanation: Ministers of State are attached to and required to assist Cabinet Ministers.

questions.question: They are usually top-level leaders of the ruling party
questions.explanation: Cabinet Ministers are usually top-level leaders of the ruling party or parties who are in charge of the major ministries.

questions.question: It is a body which includes all the ministers -
questions.explanation: Council of Ministers is the official name for the body that includes all the Ministers. It usually has 60 to 80 Ministers of different ranks.

questions.question:
Can Prime Minister appoint a person as a minister if he/she is not a member of House ?
A. Yes but such a person has to get elected to one of the House of the Parliament within six month of appointment as minister.
B. No, Prime Minister can appoint only those members who are member of either House of the Parliament.

options.html: Both A and B are false
Both A and B are true
A is false but B is true
A is true but B is false

questions.explanation:
The Prime Minister is free to choose ministers, as long as they are members of Parliament.Sometimes, a person who is not a member of Parliament can also become a minister. But such a person has to get elected to one of the Houses of the Parliament within six months of appointment as minister.

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question: What is the tenure of Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: The Prime Minister does not have a fixed tenure. He continues in power so long as he remains the leader of the majority party or coalition.

questions.question:
Read the statements and choose the correct answer.
A. The President appoints the leader of the majority party or the coalition as the Prime Minister.
B. If no party or alliance wins majority that he/she can appoint person of his/her choice as the Prime Minister.

options.html: A is true but B is false
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true
Both A and B are true

questions.explanation:
A. The President appoints the leader of the majority party or the coalition of parties that commands a majority in the Lok Sabha, as Prime Minister.
B. In case no single party or alliance gets a majority, the President appoints the person most likely to secure a majority support

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question: Who appoints the Prime Minister?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister is the most important political institution in the country. Yet there is no direct election to the post of the Prime Minister. The President appoints the Prime Minister.

questions.question: Which of the following is the most important political institution in the country?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister is the real head of Cental Government and he/she is the most important political institution in the country.

questions.question:
Read the statements and choose the correct answer.
A. A minister is more powerful than a civil servant because he is elected by the people.
B. A civil servant is more powerful than a minister as he is more educated than the minister.

options.html: A is false but B is true
Both A and B are false
A is true but B is false
Both A and B are true

questions.explanation: The minister more powerful than the civil servant because in a democracy the will of the people is supreme. The minister is elected by the people and thus empowered to exercise the will of the people on their behalf.

questions.question: Political leaders come under
questions.explanation: A politician who is elected by the people for a specific period is called the political executive. Political leaders who take big decisions fall into this category.

questions.question: Choose the right word - The functionaries who take a day to day decision.
questions.explanation: At different levels of any government we find functionaries who take day-to-day decisions and implement those decisions on behalf of the people.

questions.question: Which of the following has the power to bring No-confidence motion?
questions.explanation: If the majority of the Loksabha members say they have ‘no confidence’ in the Council of Ministers, all ministers including the Prime Minister, have to quit. The Rajyasabha does not have this power.

questions.question: For how many days the Rajya Sabha can delay a money bill?
questions.explanation: Loksabha exercises more powers in money matters. Once the Loksabha passes the budget of the government or any other money-related law, the Rajyasabha cannot reject but can only delay it by 14 days.

questions.question: Which of the following enjoys more power in case of money bill?
questions.explanation: Lok Sabha exercises more powers in money matters. Once the Loksabha passes the budget of the government or any other money related law, the Rajya Sabha cannot reject but can only delay it by 14 days.

questions.question: What happens if both the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha have different opinions on an ordinary law?

options.html: The President calls for a joint session of both the houses.
The Prime Minister has the veto power.
They will of the Lok Sabha prevails.
The President has the veto power.

questions.explanation: Any ordinary law needs to be passed by both the Houses. But if there is a difference between the two Houses, the final decision is taken in a joint session in which the view of the Loksabha is likely to prevail.

questions.question:
Read the statement and choose the correct answer.
A. Lok Sabha is more powerful than the rajya Sabha
B. Both Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha have equal power.

options.html: Both A and B are true
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true
A is true but B is false

questions.explanation:
Loksabha is more powerful than Rajyasabha because:
1.Any ordinary law needs to be passed by both the Houses. But if there is a difference between the two Houses, the final decision is taken in a joint session in which the view of the Loksabha is likely to prevail.
2. Loksabha exercises more powers in money matters.
3. Most importantly, the Loksabha controls the Council of Ministers.

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question: All laws made in the House come into force only after they receive the assent of the ________.
questions.explanation: Any bill which is passed by the Parliament needs the signature of the President to become a law.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to the President of India ?
A. The President is part of the Parliament.
B. He/She is a member of either House.

options.html: A is false but B is true
Both A and B are true
A is true but B is false
Both A and B are false

questions.explanation:
A. The President is the head of the State. The President is elected by all the Members of Parliament (MPs) and Members of State Legislative Assemblies (MLAs).

B. President should not be a member of any of the Houses of the Parliament or State Legislative Assembly.

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question: Which of the following is also known as the council of states?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is the upper house of Parliament and it consists of the representatives of the States and Union Territories and persons nominated by the President of India. Hence, it is also called the Council of States.

questions.question: Which House of the Parliament is elected indirectly?
questions.explanation: Rajya Sabha is usually elected indirectly and performs some special functions.

questions.question: Which is the highest forum of discussion and debate?
questions.explanation: Parliament is the highest forum of discussion and debate on public issues and national policy in any country. Parliament can seek information about any matter.

questions.question: Which is the final authority for making laws in India?
questions.explanation: Parliament is the final authority for making laws in any country. Parliaments all over the world can make new laws, change existing laws, or abolish existing laws and make new ones in their place.

questions.question: What is Parliament?

options.html: An organ of government which provides justice.
A national assembly of elected representatives.

questions.explanation: An assembly of elected representatives which exercises supreme political authority on behalf of the people. In India, such a national assembly of elected representatives is called Parliament.

questions.question:
‘Democratic governments insist on institutions’. Choose the appropriate answer.
A. Institutions make it difficult to rush through a bad decision.
B. They provide an opportunity for a wider set of people to be consulted in any decision.

options.html: Both A and B are false
A is true but B is false
A is false but B is true
Both A and B are true

questions.explanation:
Presence of various institutions ensures that a broad consensus is arrived at before any 
major decision is taken. Institutions also prevent a bad decision being rushed into

questions.question: Which of the following take all important policy decisions?

options.html: The Prime Minister and the President.
The Prime Minister and the Cabinet.

questions.explanation: The Prime Minister and the Cabinet ministers take all important policy decisions.

questions.question:
Choose the appropriate answer.
A. The cabinet decide to give 27% job reservation to backward classes.
B. Parliament upheld reservation as invalid.

options.html: Both A and B are true
A is true but B is false
Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true

questions.explanation: The Commission gave its Report in 1980 and made many recommendations. One of these was that 27 per cent of  government jobs be reserved for the socially and economically backward classes. For several years, many parlians and parties kept demanding the implementation of the Commission’s recommendations

questions.question: Which of the following organization have the power to settle disputes arising out of governmental decisions?

options.html: The supreme Court and the High Courts.

questions.explanation: In case of disputes arising out of governmental decisions the Supreme Court and the High Courts in India settles it.

questions.question: Which of the following party promised to implement the Mandal Commission report?
questions.explanation: In the election manifesto, the Janata Dal promised that if voted to power, it would implement the Mandal Commission report.

questions.question: Who exercises all government powers?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister is the head of the government and actually exercises all governmental powers.

questions.question: The Mandal Commission Report was associated with _______.

options.html: Reforms in the formation of government.
Reservation of seats for SCs and STs in government jobs.

questions.explanation: The Government of India had appointed the Second Backward Classes Commission in 1979. The Commission gave its Report in 1980 and made many recommendations. One of these was that 27 percent of government jobs be reserved for the socially and economically backward classes.

questions.question: Who is the head of the government?
questions.explanation: Prime Minister is the head of the government and actually exercises all governmental powers. He takes most of the decisions in the cabinet meetings.

questions.question: Who is the head of the state in India?
questions.explanation: President is the head of the state and is the highest formal authority in the country.

questions.question: What is an office memorandum?

options.html: Order issued by the Government of India.
Order issued by the president of India.

questions.explanation: On August 13, 1990, the Government of India issued an Order. It was called an Office Memorandum. Basically it is the communication issued by an appropriate authority stating policy or decision of the government.

questions.question: What is SEBC?
questions.explanation: Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBC) is the name for all those people who belong to castes that are considered backward by the government.

questions.question: Who is the Supreme Commander of the defence forces of India ?
questions.explanation: President

questions.question: The Prime Minister and the Cabinet are institutions that take care of
questions.explanation: all important policy decisions

questions.question: Which institution resolved the dispute regarding the Mandal Commission?
questions.explanation: There were lots of disputes connected regarding the Mandal Commission. But such disputes came to an end and finally Supreme Court resolved the matter.

questions.question: In which year did the Mandal Commission give its Report?
questions.explanation: The Mandal Commission gave its Report in 1980 and made many recommendations. One of these was that 27 percent of government jobs be reserved for the socially and educationally backward classes.

questions.question: Who is the real head of the government?
questions.explanation: As the real head of the government, the Prime Minister has wide ranging powers.

questions.question: The full form of SEBC is
questions.explanation: Socially and Educationally Backward Classes (SEBC) is the name for all those people who belong to castes that are considered backward by the government.

questions.question: Which of the following institutions can make changes to an existing law of the country?
questions.explanation: Parliaments all over the world can make newlaws, change existing laws, or abolish existing laws and make new ones in their place.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the judiciary is false?

options.html: Judiciary can strike down a law if it goes against the spirit of the Constitution
Every law passed by the Parliament needs approval of the Supreme Court
Any citizen can approach the courts if her rights are violated
Judiciary is independent of the Executive

questions.explanation: When a Bill has been passed by the Houses of Parliament, it shall be presented to the President, and the President shall declare either that he assents to the Bill.

questions.question: Who among the following is a part of the political executive?

options.html: Secretary of the Ministry of Home Affairs

questions.explanation: Politician who is elected by the people for a specific period is called the political executive. Political leaders who take the big decisions fall in this category. Eg.: Home Minister

questions.question: If you are elected as the President of India which of the following decision can you take on your own?

options.html: Ask for reconsideration of a bill passed by both the Houses.
Select the person you like as Prime Minister.
Dismiss a Prime Minister who has a majority in Lok Sabha.
Nominate the leaders of your choice to the Council of Ministers.

questions.explanation:
A bill passed by the Parliament becomes
a law only after the President gives assent to it. If the President can delay this for sometime if required and send the bill back to the Parliament for reconsideration.

questions.question: When is the Human Rights day celebrated?
questions.explanation: 10th December

questions.question: How was the massacre of Albanians finally stopped?

options.html: Several countries intervened to stop this massacre
Serbs and Albanians note to live happily together
Both Serbs and Albanians started to live happily together

questions.explanation: Several countries intervened to stop this massacre

questions.question: Right to vote is which type of right?
questions.explanation: Political Right

questions.question: Under the PIL any citizen or group of citizens can approach ________ for the protection of public interest against a particular law or action of the government.
questions.explanation: Supreme court

questions.question: What is the meaning of traffic in human beings?

options.html: Selling and buying of human beings usually women for immoral purposes.
A practice where the worker is forced to render service to the master free of charge.
Selling and purchasing of final goods.

questions.explanation: Selling and buying of human beings usually women for immoral purposes.

questions.question: The Indian Constitution prescribes Fundamental Rights in:
questions.explanation: Part III

questions.question: Which of the following is not a Fundamental Right?
questions.explanation: Right to vote

questions.question: Which of the following is the foundation of any democracy?
questions.explanation: Rule of law

questions.question: What is meant by Rights?

options.html: A Claim of a person over other fellow beings over the society and over the government
A Claim of a person over other countries

questions.explanation: A Claim of a person over other fellow beings over the society and over the government.

questions.question: How was the massacre of Albanians finally stopped?

options.html: Several countries intervened to stop this massacre
Both Serbs and Albanians started to live happily together
Serbs and Albanians note to live happily together

questions.explanation: Several countries intervened to stop this massacre

questions.question: What was Milosevic’s attitude towards the Albanians?

options.html: He wanted Serbs to dominate the Albanians.
His government was hostile to the Kosovo Albanians.
Albanians should either leave the country or accept the dominance of the Serbs.

questions.explanation: All of these

questions.question: What is the position of women in Saudi Arabia?

options.html: Women are subjected to many public restrictions
Women are given equal status with men
Women are given all the rights

questions.explanation: Women are subjected to many public restrictions

questions.question: Which of the following is an essential feature of any democracy?
questions.explanation: Freedom of speech and expression

questions.question: In which country there is no freedom of religion?
questions.explanation: Saudi Arabia

questions.question: Which of the following international human rights organisation collected information on the condition of the prisoners in Guantanamo Bay?
questions.explanation: Amnesty International

questions.question: How many people were secretly picked up by the US forces from all over the world and put in a prison in Guantanamo Bay?

Match the following :

1 2

A. Right to reside in any part of the country.

B. The government shall not discriminate anyone on the grounds of religion, caste, ethnicity etc.

C. The seeks the enforcement of all the rights. 

 1. Right to Equality

2. Right to Constitutional Remedies

3. Right to freedom
questions.explanation:
A.Right to Freedom:

Under the Indian Constitution as per Right to freedom ,all citizens have the right to Reside in any part of the country.

B.Right to Equality:

The government shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth.

C.Right to Constitutional Remedies:

If rights are like guarantees, they are of no use if there is no one to honour them. The fundamental rights in the Constitution are important because they are enforceable. We have a right to seek the enforcement of the above mentioned rights. This is called the Right to Constitutional Remedies.

questions.question: Indian Constitution provides us right to ________
questions.explanation:
The follwing rights are inclided in our Constituion:

  • Right to Equality.
  • Right to Freedom.
  • Right against Exploitation.
  • Right to Freedom of Religion.
  • Cultural and Educational Rights.
  • Right to Constitutional Remedies.

questions.question: Which of the following freedoms is not available to an Indian citizen ?

options.html: Freedom to start a movement to change the government.
Freedom to participate in armed revolution.
Freedom to oppose certain laws of the Constitution

questions.explanation: Citizens have the freedom to hold meetings, processions, rallies and demonstrations on any issue.They may want to discuss a problem, exchange ideas, mobilise public support to a cause, or seek votes for a candidate or party in an election. But such meetings have to be peaceful. They should not lead to public disorder or breach of peace in society. Those who participate in these activities and meetings should not carry weapons with them.Hence Freedom to participate in armed revolution is not availabe to Indian Citizen

questions.question: Choose the right term: Selling and buying of men, women, or children for immoral purposes.
questions.explanation: The Constitution prohibits ‘traffic in human beings’. Traffic here means selling and buying of human beings, usually women, for immoral purposes.

questions.question: Which of the following is a Constitutional right?
questions.explanation: Rights are not limited only to Fundamental Rights as enumerated in the Constitution. Constitution provides many more rights, which may not be Fundamental Rights. For example, the right to property is not a Fundamental Right but it is a constitutional right.

questions.question: Which of the following is a fundamental right?
questions.explanation:
Our Constitution provides for six Fundamental Rights:

  • Right to Equality.
  • Right to Freedom.
  • Right against Exploitation.
  • Right to Freedom of Religion.
  • Cultural and Educational Rights.
  • Right to Constitutional Remedies.

questions.question: Choose the right term: A formal document containing an order of the court to the government issued only by High Court or the Supreme Court.
questions.explanation: A formal document containing an order of the court to the government issued only by the High Court or the Supreme Court.

questions.question: Who called the Right to Constitutional Remedies as the heart and soul of our Constitution?
questions.explanation: If it is a Fundamental Right we can directly approach the Supreme Court or the High Court of a state. That is why Dr. Ambedkar called the Right to Constitutional Remedies, ‘the heart and soul’ of our Constitution.

questions.question: What is PIL?
questions.explanation: Any person can go to court against the violation of the Fundamental Right, if it is of social or public interest. It is called Public Interest Litigation (PIL).

questions.question: Which Fundamental Right is called the heart and soul of our Constitution?
questions.explanation: When any of our rights are violated we can seek a remedy through courts. If it is a Fundamental Right we can directly approach the Supreme Court or the High Court of a state. That is why Dr. Ambedkar called the Right to Constitutional Remedies, ‘the heart and soul’ of our Constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following Right seek the enforcement of fundamental rights?
questions.explanation: If rights are like guarantees, they are of no use if there is no one to honour them. The fundamental rights in the Constitution are important because they are enforceable. Everyone has a right to seek the enforcement of fundamental rights. This is called the Right to Constitutional Remedies.

questions.question:
Which of the following associated with Cultural and Educational Right?

  1. Any section of citizens with a distinct language or culture have a right to conserve it.
  2. Admission to any educational institution maintained by the government or receiving government aid cannot be denied to any citizen on the ground of religion or language.
  3. All minorities have the right to establish and administer educational institutions of their choice.
  4. Right to profess, practice, and propagate the religion.
questions.explanation: Every person has a right to profess, practice, and propagate the religion he or she believes in. This is included in Right to Freedom of Religion and not Cultural and Educational Right. Hence, only (i), (ii) and (iii) are true.

questions.question: Any section of citizens with a distinct language or culture have a right to conserve it. Identify the right.
questions.explanation: The Constitution specifies the cultural and educational rights of the minorities that any section of citizens with a distinct language or culture have a right to conserve it.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to ‘Right to Freedom of Religion’ ?

A. Every person has a right to profess and propagate the religion he/she believes in.

B. All are free to sacrifice animals as offerings to supernatural forces or gods.

options.html: Both A and B are false
A is false but B is true
Both A and B are true
A is true but B is false

questions.explanation:
A.Right to freedom includes right to freedom of religion as well.Every person has a right to
profess, practice and propagate the religion he or she believes in.

B.Freedom to practice religion does not mean that a person can do whatever he wants in the name of religion. For example, one cannot sacrifice animals or human beings as offerings to supernatural forces or gods. 

Hence A is true but B is false

questions.question: No one can employ a child below the age of 14 to work in any factory or mine in any hazardous work, with which law the mentioned statement is associated ?
questions.explanation: Right against Exploitation prohibits child labour. No one can employ a child below the age of fourteen to work in any factory or mine or in any other hazardous work, such as railways and ports.

questions.question: ________ is a practice where the worker is forced to render service to the master free of charge or a nominal remuneration.
questions.explanation: Begar is a practice where the worker is forced to render service to the ‘master’ free of charge or at a nominal remuneration. When this practice takes place on a life-long basis, it is called the practice of bonded labour. Our Constitution also prohibits forced labour or begar in any form.

questions.question: Which of the following is not associated with the right against exploitation?

options.html: Prohibition of traffic in human beings.

questions.explanation:
The Right against Exploitation is one of the fundamental rights. It is described in Articles 23 and 24 of the Constitution of India. The Constitution mentions three specific evils and declares these illegal.

  1. The Constitution prohibits ‘traffic in human beings’
  2. Our Constitution also prohibits forced labour or begar in any form.
  3. The Constitution also prohibits child labour.

Prohibition of untouchability comes under the right to equality, Article 17 of the Indian constitution.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a violation of the right to freedom?

options.html: Ban on use of loud speakers after 10 P.M.
Government is not allowing the opposition to hold a rally.
A dacoit of Chambal was not allowed to consult a lawyer after his arrest.
A political leader was arrested when he criticize the government.

questions.explanation: Every citizen has the right to all the freedoms. That means everyone cannot exercise freedom in such a manner that violates others’ right to freedom. Freedoms given should not cause public nuisance or disorder. Everyone is free to do everything which injures no one else. Freedom is not an unlimited licence to do what one wants. Accordingly, the government can impose certain reasonable restrictions on our freedoms in the larger interests of society. Hence, the ban on the use of loudspeakers after 10 P.M.is not a violation of the right to freedom.

questions.question: A person who is arrested and detained shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of ________ of his/her arrest.
questions.explanation: A person cannot be arrested by the police without proper legal sanction. In case of an arrest, the police have to follow one such provision: A person who is arrested and detained shall be produced before the nearest magistrate within a period of 24 hours of arrest

questions.question:
Which of the following is associated with the Right to Freedom?

  1. Freedom of speech and expression.
  2. Freedom to form associations and unions.
  3. Freedom to reside in any part of the country.
  4. Freedom to form a political party.
questions.explanation:
Under the Indian Constitution, all citizens have the right to: 

  1. Freedom of speech and expression.
  2. Assembly in a peaceful manner.
  3. Form associations and unions.
  4. Move freely throughout the country
  5. Reside in any part of the country, and
  6. Practice any profession, or to carry on any occupation, trade, or business.

questions.question: The government shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds of religion, caste, ethnicity, sex or place of birth. Mention the right with which the given statement is associated.
questions.explanation: As per the Right to equality. The government shall not discriminate against any citizen on grounds only of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth. Every citizen shall have access to public places like shops, restaurants, hotels, and cinema halls. Similarly, there shall be no restriction with regard to the use of wells, tanks, bathing ghats, roads, playgrounds, and places of public resorts maintained by the government or dedicated to the use of the general public.

questions.question: What is a Secular State?

options.html: A state that recognize only one religion.
A state that has no religion.
A secular state is one that does not establish any one religion as official religion.

questions.explanation: Secularism is the belief that no one should be discriminated on the basis of the religion he or she practises. Every citizen, irrespective of which religion he or she follows, is equal in the eyes of the laws and policies that govern the Nation. A secular state is one that does not establish any one religion as an official religion.

questions.question: Which of the following is associated with the Right against Exploitation?

options.html: The Constitution prohibits traffic in human beings.

questions.explanation:
The Constitution mentions three specific evils and declares these illegal: 

  1. The Constitution prohibits ‘traffic in human beings’.
  2. It prohibits forced labour or begar in any form.
  3. It also prohibits child labour.

questions.question:
Mr. Singh’s application was rejected as the post was reserved for SC candidates but he belonged to General Category. Which of the following is true with reference to this. A. His fundamental right. i.e., Right to Equality is violated.

B. Government has the right to reserve some posts for SC and ST candidates.

options.html: A is true but B is false
Both A and B are true
A is false but B is true
Both A and B are false

questions.explanation:
All citizens have equality of opportunity in matters relating to employment or appointment to any position in the government. No
citizen shall be discriminated against or made ineligible for employment.But the Government of India has provided reservations
for Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes and Other Backward Classes.These reservations are not against the right to equality.For
equality does not mean giving everyone the same treatment, no matter what they need. Equality means giving everyone an equal
opportunity to achieve whatever one is capable of. Sometimes it is necessary to give special treatment to someone in order to ensure equal opportunity. This is what job reservations do.To clarify this,the Constitution says that reservations of this kind are not a violation of the Right to Equality.

questions.question: Mr. Sam was denied admission in Government Medical College as he was a Christian. Which of his fundamental right is being violated?
questions.explanation: As per Cultural and Educational Right, admission to any educational institution maintained by the government or receiving government aid cannot be denied to any citizen on the ground of religion or language.

questions.question:
Read the statement and identify the fundamental law.
The constitution says that the government shall not deny to any person in India equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws.
questions.explanation: The Constitution says that the government shall not deny to any person in India equality before the law or the equal protection of the laws. It means that the laws apply in the same manner to all, regardless of a person’s status.

questions.question: How many Fundamental Rights are guaranteed to the citizens by our Constitution?
questions.explanation:
1. Right to Equality
  2. Right to Freedom
  3. Right against Exploitation
  4. Right to Freedom of Religion
  5. Cultural and Educational Rights
  6. Right to Constitutional Remedies

questions.question: The rights which are fundamental to our life and are given special status are known as ________.
questions.explanation: In India, like most other democracies in the world, these rights are mentioned in the Constitution. Some rights which are fundamental to our life are given a special status. They are called Fundamental Rights.

questions.question:
Which of the following is true with reference to Kosovo ?

A. Milosevic wanted the serbs to dominate the country.

B. His government committed atrocities on Albanians.

options.html: Both A and B are true
A is true but B is false
A is false but B is true
Both A and B are false

questions.explanation:
A. A narrow minded Serb nationalist Milosevic had won the election. His government was very hostile to the Kosovo Albanians. He wanted the Serbs to dominate the country.
B. Many Serb leaders thought that Ethnic minorities like Albanians should either leave the country or accept the dominance of the Serbs.

Hence A is true but B is false
